attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for routine follow-up of her bronchiectasis. She reports increased sputum production over the past three months. She has a medical history of ulcerative colitis diagnosed eight years ago, gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and osteoarthritis. Her medications include mesalazine, omeprazole, perindopril, metformin, and paracetamol as needed. She is a non-smoker and works as a librarian.

On examination, she appears well with pulse rate 72/min, blood pressure 135/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Chest auscultation reveals coarse crackles at both lung bases. A recent high-resolution CT chest confirms cylindrical bronchiectasis in both lower lobes.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following conditions (if any) are a recognised risk factor for bronchiectasis? (Select all that apply)
attributes.explanation:
This patient has bronchiectasis with several comorbidities, some of which are recognised risk factors for its development. Understanding these associations is important for comprehensive patient management and screening.

Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease is a recognised risk factor for bronchiectasis. It contributes to bronchiectasis through recurrent microaspiration of gastric contents, leading to chronic airway inflammation and structural damage. Studies have shown that patients with bronchiectasis have a higher prevalence of reflux disease, and optimal management of reflux may help slow disease progression.

Ulcerative colitis is also a recognised risk factor for bronchiectasis. Inflammatory bowel disease is associated with bronchiectasis as an extraintestinal manifestation, occurring in approximately 1-3% of patients. The mechanism involves shared inflammatory pathways and immune dysregulation.

Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a common chronic condition but is not a recognised risk factor for bronchiectasis development. While diabetes can increase susceptibility to respiratory infections and may complicate the management of bronchiectasis, it does not directly cause the structural airway changes seen in bronchiectasis.

Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease with no known pulmonary associations. It is an age-related condition that has no established causal relationship with bronchiectasis.

Hypertension is another common chronic condition that has no direct association with bronchiectasis development. While it may coexist with bronchiectasis, particularly in older patients, it is not considered a causative factor.

Other important risk factors for bronchiectasis include previous severe respiratory infections (particularly in childhood), immunodeficiency states, cystic fibrosis, primary ciliary dyskinesia, and connective tissue diseases. Recognition of these associations helps guide appropriate screening and management strategies in patients with multiple comorbidities.
learningPoints: Inflammatory bowel disease is associated with bronchiectasis through shared inflammatory pathways and extraintestinal manifestations.
Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease can lead to bronchiectasis through recurrent microaspiration and chronic airway inflammation.
Common conditions like hypertension and osteoarthritis are not risk factors for bronchiectasis development.
references: Chang AB, Bell SC, Byrnes CA, Dawkins P, Holland AE, Kennedy E, et al. Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand (TSANZ) position statement on chronic suppurative lung disease and bronchiectasis in children, adolescents and adults in Australia and New Zealand. Respirol Carlton Vic. 2023 Apr;28(4):339–49.
Chalmers JD, Chang AB, Chotirmall SH, Dhar R, McShane PJ. Bronchiectasis. Nature reviews Disease primers. 2018 Nov 15;4(1):45.
